title: What India Is Building vs. What It Could Build
thesis: India's digital economy has produced valuable convenience services, but a disproportionate share of capital and talent is going toward aggregation rather than deep tech, foundational AI, and scientific infrastructure.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: India's digital economy contributes roughly 11.74% of GDP, led by services and consumer platforms.

evidence.snippet: MeitY/ICRIER estimates India's digital economy at 11.74% of GDP, with services and consumer platforms contributing the largest shares.

counterevidence.summary: The 11.74% figure is an estimate based on a specific methodology; different definitions of the digital economy produce different shares, and the services-heavy composition is consistent across methodologies.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: A large share of venture funding and startup talent has gone into delivery, ride-hailing, and quick commerce.

evidence.snippet: Bain-IVCA India Venture Capital Report 2025: consumer technology was the largest VC sector in 2024, with funding rising 2.3x to US$5.4B of US$13.7B total, led by B2C commerce and quick commerce.

counterevidence.summary: Sector classifications vary across reports, and some consumer platforms also build logistics, payments, and data infrastructure that benefits other sectors.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): Bain-IVCA India VC Report 2025 confirms consumer tech (delivery, quick commerce, mobility) was the largest VC sector in 2024 at US$5.4B of US$13.7B.

claims.claim: Semiconductors, foundational AI models, scientific instruments, and industrial automation receive far smaller shares of capital and attention.

evidence.snippet: NASSCOM deep-tech reporting finds deep tech a growing but small fraction of India's startup ecosystem (under ~3% of startups), with semiconductor, AI infrastructure, and industrial automation segments attracting limited venture capital relative to consumer technology.
Global AI Index data shows that foundational AI model development is concentrated in a small number of countries and institutions, with India underrepresented in frontier model training.

counterevidence.summary: India has active public initiatives such as IndiaAI and semiconductor incentives, and deep-tech funding is growing from a small base; the gap is relative, not absolute.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): nasscom deep-tech reporting and Stanford HAI AI Index confirm deep tech/semiconductors/foundational AI get a small share of capital vs consumer tech.

claims.claim: National AI readiness depends on shifting some fraction of talent and capital toward harder, slower problems.

evidence.snippet: Navigate the tech terrain with nasscom's latest reports, the go-to resource for industry insiders and innovators.
AI readiness indices emphasize compute, data, talent, and institutional capacity as prerequisites for participating in the frontier.

counterevidence.summary: Some countries have built strong AI ecosystems through applied innovation and platform adoption without leading in foundational models; readiness can be defined in multiple ways.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

sources.title: MeitY/ICRIER: Estimation and Measurement of India's Digital Economy

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and distinguish core claims from the argument claim.
Preserve the portfolio-level framing; do not treat the article as a critique of individual companies.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not generalize beyond the public evidence.
Flag funding and sector-share figures as directional estimates rather than precise census data.
Route questions about AI extraction risks to the relevant later chapter and questions about demographics to the preceding chapter.
The article includes a venture-funding-by-sector SVG visualization inserted after Claim C2; preserve the image path, alt text, and caption when editing.

reviews.notes: Re-approved by maintainer after the meta#61 P2 series migration (hardcoded kicker strip + arc reorder; no prose change beyond the kicker line; issue #124 instruction).

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.

disclosure.note: Researched, drafted, and structured with AI agents; approved for publication by a human reviewer.
